On Facebook, right? So looking through some questions and I noticed like again, someone came up with a question that like you would think they would kind of get this by now. So I guess maybe it's my fault like that. I'm still not having you break through with understanding like what the straight line really is about at the highest level. Someone's saying, you know, I sell financial products or you know, I'm involved with money and how do I use the straight line to sort of, you know, create an airtight logical case. Well, I mean, hello is invented for financial products and for money. But you know, I think what's happening here is I think there is some confusion here about what an airtight logical case consists of and what an airtight emotional case consists of. So let me just kind of demystify that and kind of happen with this home one more time. Because when I talk about what the straight line is at the highest level, what it really is, it's essentially the seamless delivery of airtight logical cases and airtight emotional cases in the perfect order. In other words, here's the deal guys. Remember, there's two types of certainty. There's logical certainty and there's emotional certainty. Right. And in order to close the highest level, you have to have both. You just do it. Here's why. Because logical certainty is about the logical mind. It's about features and benefits, a cost benefit ratio of value proposition. When you make an airtight logical case to someone, they should be able to sort of go from A to Z or A to Z depending on where you live in the world. Right. And they could sort of connect all the dots and have an understanding of why what you're selling or recommending is empirically awesome. Like it just makes sense. It's not like there's no like secret sauce that can't be explained like it's this, it's that, it's step, step, step, right? No gaps in the middle. That causes two things to happen. Number one, a person, the individual who you're trying to sell to, they understand themselves. They get it. They understand at a very high level why it's a great deal. And they, more importantly, then can now explain it to someone else. You see, when you have made an airtight logical case to someone, if that person gets challenged by their partner, their wife, their friend, why just, oh, I did it because A, B, they can explain themselves. And there's a certain level of comfort that someone has when they can actually say, oh yeah, I made this decision because it makes sense. And here's why. So when you make the airtight logical case at a really high level, not only does someone understand themselves, but they're confident in their ability to explain to someone else. And because of that, they don't worry about being challenged or making a bad decision or if God forbid someone says, oh, why do you do your credit, you get it? So it eliminates embarrassment and so forth. So that's your logical case. It's A, B, C, one plus one, two. It's in a straight line. It all adds up. So it just, yeah, it makes sense. That's the best way to explain it. It's the numbers. It's the equation. It's the scream from the hilltops, undeniable logic of why something is a great deal, period, end of story. That's the logical case. The emotional case is very different. It's simply about skipping over all that stuff, all the minutiae, jumping to the end point and saying, yeah, imagine it already works. We already got past that. And now the person sees themselves in the future using your product, good or surface or enjoying the benefit of that product, good or service and feeling good. They're actually living the benefit in the future. Their pain has been resolved. If they were out of control in some aspect of their life, they feel in control. If they were worrying about something, they no longer worried. That's emotional certainty. And again, that worry which uses future pacing, meaning that they are able to jump past the minutiae, the A, B, C, if it was, they say, yep, it just works. I got that. When we pass that now and imagine how great I feel six months from now, driving down the road with the top down, the wind blowing my hair for the car, or having the insurance policy and knowing that my family is covered no matter what happens to me, the risks have been removed and so forth. So that so a great airtight logical case is the explanation step by step. And the airtight emotional case is when the person visualizes their life down the road already enjoying the benefit and feeling good. What happens that in the absence of making the airtight logical case, you just focus on the emotional case, the logical mind serves as a human bullshit detector. So if you try to impulse someone without essentially making the case of why it just yes, you're right. It makes sense. They're mind saying bullshit, bullshit. He's telling me how great I'm going to feel in six months from now, but he didn't convince me that it's actually going to happen. So this sort of convinces someone it's going to, yeah, it is going to happen. And this is how you feel. This is the one says this will happen. And here's how you're going to feel. You get it? This makes it happen. This is how you feel after it happens. And those two together. So what happens is once you've made this screen from the hilltops airtight, logical case. And the reason I use that screen from the hilltops is that I mean, it's just it's got to be at this really high level. And just that takes work. That takes forethought. That takes sitting down and doing research sometimes and really taking time to plan and say, how is how is the best possible way? How can I explain this to someone else? So they get it. So they say, you know, oh, wow, I get you all bullshit. No hype or bullshit aside. This just makes sense. That's a logical case. Once you've made that it then frees someone up to be moved emotionally. The bullshit detector is silenced. So once that's been silenced, you can now easily sweep someone away with their emotions imagining how great they feel down the road. Without the logic case being satisfied, what happens? They'll start to feel good. The best bullshit in the back of the mind up. Wait, someone's trying to take advantage of me and be impulse bullshit. That's why you have to have both. So in practical terms, what does that mean? Well, for me, when I know I'm doing my job, I know that I'm close. When I say to someone after I run my first loop and I made my second, I made my initial presentation, right? I frame my airtight logical case. They say they want to think about it. So let me slow this down now for a second and so watch. So I'm in the sale where I've already completed the front half of the sale. Is your straight line open, close your boundaries above and below? I finished my front half by asking for the order for the first time. I asked for the order and I was hit with a typical objection of let me think about it. That was the objection I got. To which I said, I hear, I didn't say you want to see my let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? Right? And I would use that perfect to know if you like the idea, you know, all things be equal, hypothetically speaking. And the person says to me, yeah, it sounds pretty good. And immediately I know that I have work to do because that level of certainty that I hear in his tonality says he's at a five or six, five or a six on the certainty scale for logical certainty is not screamed from the hilltop. Aerotype logic. It's it's so insane. Yeah, it kind of makes sense to me, but like I'm not, you know, I'm not really convinced yet. So when someone says that to me, what do I do? I enter their world with it. I say exactly. It really is a great deal. So I enter their world where they are. Same level. So do my own tonality. I then loop back into presentation mode. I make that second print a follow up presentation and the follow up presentation is even more poignant, more pointy. I'm like the first one. It builds on the first one. And by the time I'm done, I have made such an undeniably logical case. When I say to them, now you see what I'm saying? John, they say, oh, no, yeah, no, now I get it. Now I get it. I'm not buying from you, but now I get it. So it's almost like they don't say the second part, but in their tonality, so we go. Yeah, I love the idea. I'm not buying from you, Jordan. But oh, yeah, now I see it. That's all I want. So those were in my mind when I asked that question. So now you see the after I make that first loop, you see what I'm saying? John, does it make sense? They say, oh, yeah, no, well, yeah, wow. Oh, yeah, get it now, Jordan. Yes, great. I say exactly. They say, I'm not going to buy from you or anything. But now that I have them in a very high level of logical certainty, the rest becomes easy. So once I've done that, I take the logical case off the table. Now it's just they say, yeah, I'll buy it. I'm not buying now because that's what the logical mind does. The logical mind always wants to know more, find out more and always wants to think about the emotional brain says, I got to have this and I got to have it now. So what I do is I create that scream from the hilltops airtight logical case up front. And then once I've established that, then my pattern start focusing on emotion, future pacing, but I'll always put a little bit of knowledge in there in terms of logic, because I want to button up that certainty because certainty.
